botability.description: Help AI and search engines discover, crawl, and index your website.
botability.standardsDescription: Standards for helping search engines and AI tools find, read, and understand your website.
botability.guidanceDescription: Policy and guidance that supports making your website discoverable by search engines and AI.
botability.why: Smart technology can better process and share public information, making services faster and more efficient for users.
botability.risk: AI tools cannot find or understand your content.

attributes.description: The site allows search engines and AI agents to index its pages.
attributes.why: Allows AI bots to find and index your pages, helping your content appear in search results and reach more people.
attributes.risk: Search engines cannot find your pages.
attributes.error: Site is not accessible to well-behaved agents.

userStories.title: As an AI bot, I want to be able to crawl and access all relevant content on the website so that I can accurately index and understand the site for providing useful responses to users.

about.title: About
about.content: The robots.txt file tells AI crawlers and search engines which parts of a website they can or cannot access. This helps website owners control what data is available to AI models while protecting private or sensitive information. It also prevents unnecessary crawling, reducing server load and improving site performance. By following robots.txt rules, AI crawlers respect website preferences and ensure ethical data collection.

code.title: Code
code.content:
robots.txt — allow all crawlers:

User-agent: *
Allow: /

HTML meta robots tag:

<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">

example.title: Example
example.content:
A minimal passing robots.txt that allows all crawlers and links to the sitemap:

User-agent: *
Allow: /

Sitemap: https://example.gov/sitemap.xml

attributes.description: The page's main content loads without requiring JavaScript.
attributes.why: Ensures search engines can read and index the content within a document, making it discoverable and improving visibility online.
attributes.risk: Bots cannot read your content.
attributes.error: Main content not available in the initial document.

userStories.title: As a user or AI bot, I want the content of the website to be accessible and available directly in the document so that I can easily access, read, and index the information without relying on external sources or heavy JavaScript execution.

about.title: About
about.content: The main content of each page is available in the initial document. It does not require a lot of client-side script execution in order to render. If page usually require a lot of client-side script execution to render main content the content may be made available to bots in an alternate text version of the site.

attributes.description: Homepage is marked up as an official government organization.
attributes.why: Identifies websites as official government agencies for AI systems and search engines to recognize automatically.
attributes.risk: Systems cannot verify you are an official government site.
attributes.error: Homepage does not use standard Schema.org code that identifies the site as a government organization.

userStories.title: As a user or bot, I want homepages owned by government organizations to identify themselves as government agencies using the standard Schema.org type definitions.

about.title: About
about.content: The GovernmentOrganization Schema.org type is present in the pages as structured data.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example JSON-LD structured data:

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "GovernmentOrganization",
  "name": "U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs",
  "url": "https://www.va.gov"
}
</script>

example.title: Example
example.content:
Complete JSON-LD with commonly used government organization properties:

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "GovernmentOrganization",
  "name": "U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs",
  "alternateName": "VA",
  "url": "https://www.va.gov",
  "logo": "https://www.va.gov/img/va-logo.png",
  "description": "The VA provides benefits, services, and honor to veterans and their families.",
  "address": {
    "@type": "PostalAddress",
    "streetAddress": "810 Vermont Avenue NW",
    "addressLocality": "Washington",
    "addressRegion": "DC",
    "postalCode": "20420",
    "addressCountry": "US"
  },
  "contactPoint": {
    "@type": "ContactPoint",
    "telephone": "+1-800-698-2411",
    "contactType": "customer service"
  },
  "sameAs": [
    "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Veterans_Affairs",
    "https://www.facebook.com/VeteransAffairs",
    "https://www.youtube.com/user/DeptVetAffairs"
  ]
}
</script>

attributes.description: The sitemap file loads and returns a successful response.
attributes.why: Confirms the sitemap is accessible, ensuring search engines can easily find and index all pages on the site.
attributes.risk: Search engines cannot find all your pages.
attributes.error: Sitemap.xml is missing or inaccessible.

about.title: About
about.content:
A sitemap is a file that lists all of the pages on a website in addition to information about each so that search engines can more intelligently crawl the site.

Web crawlers usually discover pages from links within the site and from other sites. Sitemaps supplement this data to allow crawlers that support sitemaps to pick up all Uniform Resources Locators (URLs) in the sitemap and learn about them using the associated metadata. Using sitemap protocol doesn’t guarantee web pages are included in search engines, but provides hints for web crawlers to do a better job of crawling your site.

The sitemap is an Extensible Markup Language (XML) file on the website’s root directory that include metadata for each URL, such as:

  • when it was last updated
  • how often it usually changes
  • how important it is, relative to other URLs in the site

code.title: Code
code.content:
XML sitemap listing pages on a site:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<urlset xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9">
  <url>
    <loc>https://example.gov/</loc>
    <lastmod>2024-01-01</lastmod>
    <changefreq>weekly</changefreq>
    <priority>1.0</priority>
  </url>
  <url>
    <loc>https://example.gov/about/</loc>
    <lastmod>2024-01-01</lastmod>
  </url>
</urlset>

Reference your sitemap from robots.txt:

User-agent: *
Allow: /

Sitemap: https://example.gov/sitemap.xml

userStories.title: As a search engine bot or user, I want the website's sitemap to be easily accessible so that I can discover all the pages and content on the site efficiently and improve the website's discoverability.

attributes.description: The sitemap file uses the required XML format.
attributes.why: Stores site structure in a readable format, helping search engines efficiently crawl and index all website pages.
attributes.risk: Search engines cannot read your sitemap.
attributes.error: Sitemap is not in XML format.

userStories.title: As a search engine bot, I want the website to provide a properly formatted XML sitemap so that I can efficiently crawl and index all relevant pages, improving the site's visibility in search results.

about.title: About
about.content:
The sitemap is an Extensible Markup Language (XML) file on the website's root directory that include metadata for each URL, such as:

  • when it was last updated
  • how often it usually changes
  • how important it is, relative to other URLs in the site

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example government website sitemaps:

  • https://www.usa.gov/sitemap.xml
  • https://18f.gov/sitemap.xml

code.title: Code
code.content:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>

<urlset xmlns="http://www.sitemaps.org/schemas/sitemap/0.9">

   <url>

      <loc>http://www.example.com/</loc>

      <lastmod>2005-01-01</lastmod>

      <changefreq>monthly</changefreq>

      <priority>0.8</priority>

   </url>

</urlset>

attributes.description: The site has a robots.txt file that guides search engines.
attributes.why: Guides search engines on which pages to crawl or avoid, ensuring important content is indexed and irrelevant pages aren't.
attributes.risk: Search engines ignore your instructions.
attributes.error: Robots.txt is missing or invalid.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content: All government websites must allow robots indexing and following.

about.title: About
about.content: Robots valid means the robots.txt file is correctly written and follows the rules that search engines understand. If it's valid, search engines can read the file and know which parts of the site they can or can't visit. A valid file helps control how your site shows up in search results and avoids errors.

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example government website robots.txt files:

  • https://www.usa.gov/robots.txt
  • https://www.ca.gov/robots.txt

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example robots.txt code:

# Only applies to search.gov scraping
User-agent: usasearch  
# Slow amount of requests
Crawl-delay: 2
# Specify it can read /archive/
Allow: /archive/

# Applies to all scrapers
User-agent: *
# Slow amount of requests to 1 every 10 seconds
Crawl-delay: 10
# Don't let them read /archive/
Disallow: /archive/

# Point to a sitemap file
Sitemap: /sitemap.xml

Example robots meta code:

<!-- This page can be indexed 
and links on it can be followed -->
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">

Example X-Robots-Tag:

# This URL can be indexed 
# and links on it can be followed
X-Robots-Tag: index, follow

attributes.description: The robots.txt file allows search engines and AI agents to crawl the site.
attributes.why: Permits search engines and web tools to access content, helping improve search visibility and gather relevant data.
attributes.risk: Your site is blocked from search results.
attributes.error: Robots policy blocks browsers or scrapers.

userStories.title: As a search engine crawler, I want to read the robots.txt file so that I know which parts of the website I am allowed or disallowed to index.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content: All government websites must allow robots indexing and following.

about.title: About
about.content: Robots allowed in a robots.txt file means search engines like Google are allowed to look at and list (or “crawl”) the page in search results. Website owners use robots.txt to tell search engines what they can and can't see. If robots are allowed, the site or page can show up in search engines, making it easier for people to find.

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example government website robots.txt files:

  • https://www.usa.gov/robots.txt
  • https://www.ca.gov/robots.txt

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example robots.txt code:

# Only applies to search.gov scraping
User-agent: usasearch  
# Slow amount of requests
Crawl-delay: 2
# Specify it can read /archive/
Allow: /archive/

# Applies to all scrapers
User-agent: *
# Slow amount of requests to 1 every 10 seconds
Crawl-delay: 10
# Don't let them read /archive/
Disallow: /archive/

# Point to a sitemap file
Sitemap: /sitemap.xml

Example robots meta code:

<!-- This page can be indexed 
and links on it can be followed -->
<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">

Example X-Robots-Tag:

# This URL can be indexed 
# and links on it can be followed
X-Robots-Tag: index, follow

attributes.description: The robots.txt file links to the sitemap.
attributes.why: Helps search engines find the sitemap quickly, improving how they discover and index website pages.
attributes.risk: Search engines take longer to find your pages.
attributes.error: robots.txt missing sitemap reference.

A robots.txt file that allows all crawlers and references the sitemap:

attributes.description: Each page declares a preferred URL so search engines show the right link.
attributes.why: Prevents duplicate content issues by telling search engines which version of a page is the main one.
attributes.risk: Search rankings split across duplicate pages.
attributes.error: Duplicate page URLs detected.

userStories.title: As a user or search engine bot, I want to be directed to the preferred version of a webpage (canonical URL) to avoid duplicate content and ensure that I am viewing the correct page with the most relevant content.

about.title: About
about.content: Canonicalization ensures that search engines index the correct version of a page when there are duplicate or similar pages. By specifying a canonical URL with the rel="canonical" tag, you direct search engines to the primary page, helping improve SEO. This prevents search engines from splitting the ranking between multiple pages with similar content. Proper canonicalization avoids indexing issues and boosts a site's performance in search results.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<link rel="canonical" href="https://example.gov/page/">

example.title: Examples
example.content:
When multiple URLs serve the same content:

<!-- All these URLs resolve to the same page -->
https://example.gov/page
https://example.gov/page/
https://www.example.gov/page/

<!-- Canonical tells search engines which is the primary URL -->
<link rel="canonical" href="https://example.gov/page/">

attributes.description: Links use descriptive text that tells users and search engines where they go.
attributes.why: Describes the link’s purpose clearly, helping users know where it leads and improving navigation for everyone.
attributes.risk: Users cannot tell where links go.
attributes.error: Links do not have descriptive text.

about.title: About
about.content: Link text is the clickable word or phrase in a hyperlink. When link text clearly conveys a hyperlink's target, both users and search engines can more easily understand your content and how it relates to other pages.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Vague: does not describe the destination -->
<a href="/report-2024.pdf">Click here</a>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Descriptive: users know where they are going -->
<a href="/report-2024.pdf">Download the 2024 annual report (PDF)</a>

attributes.description: Pages declare their language and region for international users.
attributes.why: Indicates page language and region, helping users see the right version and improving search results in different countries.
attributes.risk: Users see pages in wrong language.
attributes.error: The pages has a missing or invalid hreflang attribute.

userStories.title: As a website visitor, I want to see the page in my language so I can easily understand the content.

about.title: About
about.content: The `hreflang` attribute helps search engines understand which language and region a webpage is targeting. This is useful when a website has multiple versions in different languages or for different regions. By using `hreflang`, the site can show the correct version of a page to users based on their language or location, improving their experience. It also helps search engines avoid showing the wrong language version in search results, leading to better SEO and user engagement.

links.title: Links
links.content:
• Document doesn't have a valid hreflang
  • Tell Google about localized versions of your page

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head> for each language version:

<link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://example.gov/en/page/">
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="es" href="https://example.gov/es/page/">
<link rel="alternate" hreflang="x-default" href="https://example.gov/page/">

example.title: Example
example.content:
Full <head> pattern for a site offering English and Spanish content:

<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Benefits — U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs</title>

  <!-- Self-referencing canonical -->
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://www.va.gov/benefits/">

  <!-- hreflang: tell search engines about language alternates -->
  <link rel="alternate" hreflang="en" href="https://www.va.gov/benefits/">
  <link rel="alternate" hreflang="es" href="https://www.va.gov/es/benefits/">
  <link rel="alternate" hreflang="x-default" href="https://www.va.gov/benefits/">
</head>

accessibility.description: Meet WCAG standards so every type of user can use your website.
accessibility.standardsDescription: Standards for making your website usable by people with disabilities.
accessibility.guidanceDescription: Policy and guidance that supports web accessibility for people with disabilities.
accessibility.why: People with disabilities need equal access to information and services online.
accessibility.risk: People with disabilities cannot use your site.

attributes.description: Each access key shortcut is unique so keyboard navigation works correctly.
attributes.why: Allows keyboard-only users and screen reader users to efficiently move through and interact with page content.
attributes.risk: Keyboard users cannot efficiently navigate your site.
attributes.error: Failed: [accesskey] values are unique

userStories.title: As a keyboard-only user, I want to efficiently navigate through page content without getting stuck or lost.

about.title: About
about.content: Access keys let users quickly focus a part of the page. For proper navigation, each access key must be unique.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: duplicate access key -->
<a href="/search" accesskey="s">Search</a>
<a href="/sitemap" accesskey="s">Sitemap</a>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: unique access keys -->
<a href="/search" accesskey="s">Search</a>
<a href="/sitemap" accesskey="m">Sitemap</a>

attributes.description: Accessibility attributes are only used where they are supported.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers ignore attributes that don't match the element's role.
attributes.error: Failed: [aria-*] attributes match their roles

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. [Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-allowed-attr).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: aria-checked is not valid on a paragraph -->
<p aria-checked="true">Status: active</p>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: aria-checked is valid on a checkbox role -->
<input type="checkbox" aria-checked="true">

attributes.displayName: Uses ARIA roles only on compatible elements
attributes.description: Accessibility roles are only applied to elements that support them.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers misinterpret elements assigned unsupported roles.
attributes.error: Failed: Uses ARIA roles only on compatible elements

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Many HTML elements can only be assigned certain ARIA roles. Using ARIA roles where they are not allowed can interfere with the accessibility of the web page.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: role="dialog" not allowed on a table caption -->
<caption role="dialog">Report data</caption>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: use an appropriate element -->
<caption>Report data</caption>

attributes.displayName: button, link, and menuitem elements have accessible names
attributes.description: Buttons, links, and menu items have labels that screen readers can announce.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers announce buttons and links without names, hiding their purpose.
attributes.error: Failed: button, link, and menuitem elements have accessible names

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. [Learn how to make command elements more accessible](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-command-name).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: menuitem with no accessible name -->
<li role="menuitem"></li>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: menuitem contains visible text -->
<li role="menuitem">Print</li>

attributes.displayName: ARIA attributes are used as specified for the element's role
attributes.description: Accessibility attributes are only used when the element's role allows them.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers ignore attributes used outside their allowed conditions.
attributes.error: Failed: ARIA attributes are used as specified for the element's role

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Some ARIA attributes are only allowed on an element under certain conditions.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: aria-expanded on a non-expandable element -->
<p aria-expanded="true">Details</p>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: aria-expanded on a button that controls a collapsible region -->
<button aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="details-panel">Details</button>
<div id="details-panel">...</div>

attributes.displayName: Deprecated ARIA roles were not used
attributes.description: Accessibility roles use current, supported values.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers may not recognize deprecated roles, breaking navigation.
attributes.error: Failed: Deprecated ARIA roles were not used

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Deprecated ARIA roles may not be processed correctly by assistive technology.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: deprecated role -->
<div role="directory">Navigation links</div>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: use a semantic element -->
<ul aria-label="Navigation links">...</ul>

attributes.displayName: Elements with role="dialog" or role="alertdialog" have accessible names
attributes.description: Dialog boxes have labels so screen readers can describe their purpose.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers cannot tell users what a dialog box is for.
attributes.error: Failed: Elements with role="dialog" or role="alertdialog" have accessible names

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. [Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-dialog-name).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: dialog with no accessible name -->
<div role="dialog">...</div>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: aria-labelledby references a visible heading -->
<div role="dialog" aria-labelledby="dialog-title">
  <h2 id="dialog-title">Confirm your submission</h2>
  ...
</div>

attributes.displayName: [aria-hidden="true"] is not present on the document <body>
attributes.description: The page body is not hidden from screen readers.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers cannot read any content on the page.
attributes.error: Failed: [aria-hidden="true"] is not present on the document <body>

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Assistive technologies, like screen readers, work inconsistently when `aria-hidden="true"` is set on the document ``. [Learn how `aria-hidden` affects the document body](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-hidden-body).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: aria-hidden on the body element -->
<body aria-hidden="true">

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: remove aria-hidden from body -->
<body>

attributes.displayName: [aria-hidden="true"] elements do not contain focusable descendents
attributes.description: Interactive elements are not hidden from screen reader users.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Keyboard users can focus elements that screen readers cannot announce.
attributes.error: Failed: [aria-hidden="true"] elements do not contain focusable descendents

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. [Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-hidden-focus).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: focusable button inside aria-hidden container -->
<div aria-hidden="true">
  <button>Close</button>
</div>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: remove aria-hidden, or make element non-focusable -->
<div aria-hidden="true">
  <button tabindex="-1">Close</button>
</div>

attributes.displayName: ARIA input fields have accessible names
attributes.description: Form input fields have labels that screen readers can announce.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers announce form fields without names, leaving users confused.
attributes.error: Failed: ARIA input fields have accessible names

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: custom textbox with no accessible name -->
<div role="textbox" contenteditable="true"></div>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: aria-label provides accessible name -->
<div role="textbox" contenteditable="true" aria-label="Search query"></div>

attributes.displayName: ARIA meter elements have accessible names
attributes.description: Meter elements have labels so screen readers can announce their value.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers cannot describe what a meter is measuring.
attributes.error: Failed: ARIA meter elements have accessible names

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About
about.content: When a meter element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. [Learn how to name `meter` elements](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/aria-meter-name).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: meter with no accessible name -->
<div role="meter" aria-valuenow="75" aria-valuemin="0" aria-valuemax="100"></div>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: aria-label names the meter -->
<div role="meter" aria-valuenow="75" aria-valuemin="0" aria-valuemax="100" aria-label="Download progress">75%</div>

attributes.displayName: ARIA progressbar elements have accessible names
attributes.description: Progress bars have labels so screen readers can describe their status.
attributes.why: Ensures assistive technologies like screen readers can correctly interpret and announce page elements to users with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Screen readers cannot tell users what a progress bar is tracking.
attributes.error: Failed: ARIA progressbar elements have accessible names

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want ARIA attributes to be correctly implemented so that I can understand the purpose and state of interactive elements.

about.title: About

attributes.displayName: Links are distinguishable without relying on color

about.content: Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. [Learn how to make links distinguishable](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/link-in-text-block).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

/* Fail: link is indistinguishable from surrounding text */
a { color: inherit; text-decoration: none; }

Passing pattern:

a { text-decoration: underline; }

/* Alternative: bold weight + color */
a { color: #0054a0; font-weight: bold; }

attributes.description: Links have unique, descriptive text so users know where they go.

about.content: Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. [Learn how to make links accessible](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/link-name).

code.title: Code

<a href="/report.pdf"><img src="pdf-icon.png" alt="Download annual report (PDF)"></a>

Failing pattern:

about.content: Users do not expect a page to refresh automatically, and doing so will move focus back to the top of the page. This may create a frustrating or confusing experience.

attributes.description: Skip links are visible and reachable by keyboard so users can bypass navigation.
attributes.why: Gives screen reader users meaningful descriptions of interactive elements so they can understand and use them.
attributes.risk: Screen reader users cannot identify or interact with elements.
attributes.error: Failed: Skip links are focusable

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want all interactive elements to have clear, descriptive names so that I know what they do.

about.title: About
about.content: Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time.

code.title: Code
code.content:
<a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>

<nav aria-label="Main navigation">
  ...
</nav>

<main id="main-content">
  ...
</main>

example.title: Example
example.content:
A visually hidden skip link that becomes visible on focus:

<style>
  .skip-link {
    position: absolute;
    top: -100%;
    left: 0;
    background: #0054a0;
    color: #fff;
    padding: 0.5rem 1rem;
    text-decoration: none;
    z-index: 1000;
  }
  .skip-link:focus {
    top: 0;
  }
</style>

<a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>

<nav>...</nav>

<main id="main-content">
  <!-- page content -->
</main>

attributes.displayName: No element has a [tabindex] value greater than 0
attributes.description: The page uses natural keyboard tab order without manual overrides.
attributes.why: Allows keyboard-only users and screen reader users to efficiently move through and interact with page content.
attributes.risk: Keyboard users cannot efficiently navigate your site.
attributes.error: No element has a [tabindex] value greater than 0

userStories.title: As a keyboard-only user, I want to efficiently navigate through page content without getting stuck or lost.

about.title: About
about.content: A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: positive tabindex values create unpredictable tab order -->
<button tabindex="3">First action</button>
<button tabindex="1">Second action</button>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: rely on DOM order; tabindex="0" to include non-interactive elements -->
<button>First action</button>
<button>Second action</button>

attributes.displayName: Tables have different content in the summary attribute and <caption>
attributes.description: Table captions and summaries have different content.
attributes.why: Helps screen readers announce structured content (tables, lists) in a logical order that users can understand.
attributes.risk: Screen readers announce structured content incorrectly.
attributes.error: Failed: Tables have different content in the summary attribute and <caption>

userStories.title: As a screen reader user, I want tables and lists to be properly structured so that I can understand the relationships between items.

about.title: About
about.content: The summary attribute should describe the table structure, while `` should have the onscreen title. Accurate table mark-up helps users of screen readers.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: caption and summary contain the same text -->
<table summary="Agency contacts">
  <caption>Agency contacts</caption>
</table>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: summary adds detail beyond the caption -->
<table summary="Columns: Name, Phone, Email, Office hours">
  <caption>Agency contacts</caption>
</table>

attributes.displayName: Touch targets have sufficient size and spacing
attributes.description: Buttons and interactive elements are large enough to tap easily on touch screens.
attributes.why: Follows established patterns that prevent common usability barriers for people with disabilities.
attributes.risk: Users with motor disabilities struggle to tap small buttons.
attributes.error: Failed: Touch targets have sufficient size and spacing

userStories.title: As a user with a disability, I want the site to follow accessibility best practices so that I can use it without encountering barriers.

about.title: About
about.content: Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

/* Fail: touch target too small */

Passing pattern:

/* Pass: meets WCAG 2.2 minimum (24x24 px) */
.icon-btn {
  min-width: 24px;
  min-height: 24px;
  padding: 0.5rem 1rem;
}

attributes.displayName: Cells in a <table> element that use the [headers] attribute refer to table cells within the same table

attributes.risk: Screen readers announce structured content incorrectly.
attributes.error: Failed: Cells in a <table> element that use the [headers] attribute refer to table cells within the same table

about.title: About
about.content: Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring `` cells using the `[headers]` attribute only refer to other cells in the same table may improve the experience for screen reader users.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: headers attribute references a non-existent id -->
<td headers="missing-id">Value</td>

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: headers references valid th ids -->
<table>
  <tr>
    <th id="col-name">Name</th>
    <th id="col-dept">Department</th>
  </tr>
  <tr>
    <td headers="col-name">Jane Smith</td>
    <td headers="col-dept">Finance</td>

attributes.displayName: <th> elements and elements with [role="columnheader"/"rowheader"] have data cells they describe

code.content:

  <tr><th>Name</th><th>Department</th><th>Notes</th></tr>
  <tr><td>Jane</td><td>Finance</td></tr>
</table>

Passing pattern:

<table>
  <tr><th>Name</th><th>Department</th></tr>
  <tr><td>Jane</td><td>Finance</td></tr>
</table>

attributes.description: Language codes on page elements are valid so screen readers pronounce content correctly.

about.content: Specifying a valid [BCP 47 language](https://www.w3.org/International/questions/qa-choosing-language-tags#question) on elements helps ensure that text is pronounced correctly by a screen reader. [Learn how to use the `lang` attribute](https://dequeuniversity.com/rules/axe/4.10/valid-lang).

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: invalid BCP 47 value -->

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: valid BCP 47 codes -->

<p lang="es">Este párrafo está en español.</p>

<video controls>

  <track kind="captions" src="captions-en.vtt" srclang="en" label="English" default>
</video>

attributes.description: Images display at their intended proportions.
attributes.why: Ensures images display correctly without distortion on different screens and devices.
attributes.risk: Images look stretched or squished.
attributes.error: Image could not be displayed correctly due to aspect ratio issues.

userStories.title: As a user, I want the website to display images at the correct aspect ratio so that I can read and interact with the content without distortion.

about.title: About
about.content: Image aspect ratio is the relationship between an image's width and height. It's expressed as two numbers, like 16:9 or 4:3. For example, a 16:9 aspect ratio means the width is 16 units wide for every 9 units of height. Aspect ratio helps ensure images look good on different screens, like phones, computers, and TVs. Using the correct ratio prevents stretching or squishing the image and maintains its quality.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: explicit dimensions that distort the image -->
<img src="logo.png" width="300" height="50">

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: set one dimension and let the other scale -->
<img src="logo.png" width="300" height="auto">

<!-- Or in CSS -->
img { max-width: 100%; height: auto; }

attributes.description: The page has a doctype declaration so browsers render it correctly.
attributes.why: Helps browsers display web pages correctly by telling them what kind of HTML code the page is using.
attributes.risk: Browsers display your page wrong.
attributes.error: Page may not display correctly due to missing or incorrect doctype declaration.

userStories.title: As a web developer, I want to define the correct doctype so that my website displays properly in all browsers.
As a website visitor, I want pages to load and look right, so I can read and use them easily.

about.title: About
about.content: Doctype (short for "document type declaration") tells the web browser what version of HTML the page is using. It helps the browser understand how to read and display the webpage correctly. Without it, the browser might not show the page the way it should. The most common doctype today is for HTML5, written as <!DOCTYPE html>. It's simple but important for making websites work the right way across different browsers.

code.title: Code
code.content: <!doctype html>

links.title: Links
links.content:
• Learn more about the doctype declaration (MDN)
  • The DOCTYPE

attributes.description: The page declares its character encoding so text displays correctly.
attributes.why: Helps computers show words and symbols correctly in different languages and on different devices.
attributes.risk: Special characters show up as weird symbols.
attributes.error: Charset declaration is missing or occurs too late in the HTML.

userStories.title: As a website visitor, I want the text to display correctly in my language so I can read and understand it easily.

about.title: About
about.content: A charset (character set) is a list of letters, numbers, and symbols that computers use to show and read text. It helps make sure the right characters appear, especially in different languages. For example, UTF-8 is a popular charset that works with many languages and symbols. Without the correct charset, websites may show weird symbols or question marks. Setting the right charset helps everyone read and understand the text properly.

code.title: Code
code.content:
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <title>Page title</title>
</head>

usability.description: Optimize content structure so users quickly find what they need.
usability.standardsDescription: Standards for making your website easy to navigate so people can find what they need.
usability.guidanceDescription: Policy and guidance that supports usable, user-friendly website design.
usability.why: Well-written pages help people understand laws, services, and procedures without confusion, reducing calls and office visits.

attributes.description: The page has a unique, descriptive title shown in browser tabs and search results.
attributes.why: Identifies the page's content, helping users and search engines understand it quickly.
attributes.risk: Users cannot tell what your page is about.
attributes.error: Page title is missing or unclear.

userStories.title: As a screen reader user or someone navigating multiple tabs, I want each page to have a clear and unique title so that I can quickly understand the content and context of the page.

about.title: About
about.content: The HTML <title> tag sets the title of a webpage, which appears in the browser tab and search results. It helps users know what the page is about and improves search engine optimization. The <title> tag goes inside the <head> section of an HTML document. A good title is short, clear, and relevant to the page content.

code.title: Code
code.content:
<head>
  <title>Our website</title>
</head>

attributes.description: The page has a meta description that summarizes its content for search results.
attributes.why: Describes page content briefly, helping users understand what to expect and improving search engine results.
attributes.risk: Search results do not show what your page does.
attributes.error: Page description is missing or unclear.

userStories.title: As a user browsing search results or sharing links, I want each page to have a clear and relevant description so that I can understand what the page is about before visiting it.

about.title: About
about.content: A page description is a short summary of a webpage, usually shown in search results. It's set using the <meta name="description"> tag inside the <head> section of an HTML document. A good description is clear, brief, and tells users what to expect on the page. It also helps with search engine optimization.

code.title: Code
code.content:
<head>
  <meta name="description" content="Welcome to our website">
</head>

attributes.description: Page content is written at a reading level most users can understand.
attributes.why: Improves communication by helping readers quickly understand messages without confusion or frustration.
attributes.risk: People struggle to understand your content.
attributes.error: Automated Readability Index score is above 8 (higher than 7th grade reading level).

userStories.title: As a user reading content on a web page, I want the text to have high readability through clear content and optimal presentation so that I can understand and process the information with minimal effort and maximum comprehension.

about.title: About
about.content: Readability measures how easy text is to understand by looking at words and layout. Experts like Flesch-Kincaid and Automated Readability Index help test reading level. For example, ARI uses the average number of characters per word and words per sentence to give a U.S. school grade level—e.g., 8 = eighth grade. Simple words, short sentences, clear design, and legible fonts make text easier to read. These tests help match writing to different audiences' skill levels.

attributes.description: The page scales correctly on mobile devices and tablets.
attributes.why: Optimizes page display for various devices, ensuring content looks good on all screen sizes and enhances user experience.
attributes.risk: Mobile users see broken pages on their phones.
attributes.error: Viewport meta tag is missing.

userStories.title: As a mobile user, I want the website to scale and display correctly on my device so that I can read and interact with the content without zooming or horizontal scrolling.

about.title: About
about.content: The viewport meta tag helps web pages look good on all devices by controlling the screen size and zoom settings. This makes websites adjust to fit different screens. You can also disable zooming or set a custom zoom level. It's important for making websites mobile-friendly.

example.title: Example
example.content: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

<!-- Fail: maximum-scale prevents users from zooming -->
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1">

Passing pattern:

<!-- Pass: allows user scaling -->
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">

attributes.description: The site works whether users type www or not.
attributes.why: Ensures consistent site display whether users type with or without 'www,' preventing errors and improving overall accessibility.
attributes.risk: Users get errors when typing your web address.
attributes.error: www and non-www versions are inconsistent.

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want to access the website using either www or non-www URLs and be automatically redirected to the correct version so that I don't encounter errors or duplicate content.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content: All government websites must have www and non-www resolution.

about.title: About
about.content: Domain canonicalization allows access to the intended website address, whether users type www (www.example.gov) or not (example.gov). Improper canonicalization will fail to direct example.gov to the intended address, and users will receive an error.

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example of a government website with www and non-www resolution:

  • https://digital.gov
  • https://www.digital.gov

code.title: Code
code.content:
nginx — redirect non-www to www (or www to non-www, pick one canonical form):

# Redirect www to non-www
server {
  listen 80;
  server_name www.example.gov;
  return 301 https://example.gov$request_uri;
}
server {
  listen 443 ssl;
  server_name www.example.gov;
  return 301 https://example.gov$request_uri;
}

attributes.description: Page content stays in place as the page loads.
attributes.why: Minimizes unexpected page movements, improving user experience by keeping content stable while it loads.
attributes.risk: Users click wrong buttons by mistake.
attributes.error: Unexpected layout shifts detected.

userStories.title: As a user reading the page, I want the content to stay stable while loading so that I don't accidentally click the wrong thing or lose my place.

about.title: About
about.content: ​Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures how much a webpage's content unexpectedly moves during loading, affecting user experience. A good CLS score is 0.1 or less. Common causes include images or ads without specified dimensions, dynamically added content, and late-loading fonts. To improve CLS, always set size attributes for media, reserve space for ads, and ensure fonts load properly. ​

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

/* Fail: no dimensions causes layout shift when image loads */
img { max-width: 100%; }

Passing pattern:

/* Pass: reserve space with aspect-ratio or explicit dimensions */
img {
  max-width: 100%;
  height: auto;
  aspect-ratio: 16 / 9;
}

attributes.description: The page starts displaying content within 1.8 seconds.
attributes.why: Measures how quickly important content appears, improving user experience by reducing wait time when loading a page.
attributes.risk: Users see blank screen and think site is broken.
attributes.error: Page didn't display within 1.8 seconds.

userStories.title: As a user visiting the website, I want the first visible content to load quickly so that I know the site is responding and I don't leave out of frustration.

videos.title: First Contentful Paint (FCP) + Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)
videos.description: Improving the performance metrics: First and Largest contentful paint on the scangov.com site.

about.title: About
about.content: First Contentful Paint (FCP) measures how quickly a webpage shows any content after a user starts to load it. It checks the time from when the user begins to navigate to the page until any part of the page's content, like text or images, appears on the screen. A fast FCP reassures users that the page is loading. For a good user experience, aim for an FCP of 1.8 seconds or less.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Inline critical CSS and defer non-critical styles:

<style>/* critical above-the-fold styles */
body { font-family: sans-serif; }
</style>
<link rel="preload" href="styles.css" as="style" onload="this.rel='stylesheet'">

attributes.description: The page responds to user interactions in under 0.2 seconds.
attributes.why: A fast response time increases interactivity and user satisfaction, leading to a more engaging experience.
attributes.risk: Site feels frozen when users click things.
attributes.error: Page response exceeded 0.2 seconds.

userStories.title: As a user interacting with the page, I want the site to respond quickly to my actions so that I feel in control and don't experience delays.

As a user of a cheaper model mobile phone, I want your website to respond to my interactions quickly. If it is inefficient and performs a lot of processing on my device I wonder if it is even working, get stressed out and may click multiple times.

about.title: About
about.content: Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures how quickly a webpage responds to user actions like clicks or typing. It tracks the time from when you interact until the next screen update. A good INP score is 200 milliseconds or less, ensuring a smooth experience. INP has replaced First Input Delay (FID) as a key performance metric.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

// Fail: long-running synchronous work on the main thread
document.getElementById("btn").addEventListener("click", () => {
  heavyComputation(); // blocks the main thread
});

Passing pattern:

// Pass: defer heavy work off the main thread
document.getElementById("btn").addEventListener("click", () => {
  setTimeout(() => heavyComputation(), 0);
});

attributes.description: The page's main content loads within 2.5 seconds.
attributes.why: Measures when the main content fully loads, improving user experience by showing the most important part of a page quickly.
attributes.risk: Users wait too long and leave your page.
attributes.error: Main content didn't load in 2.5 seconds.

about.title: About
about.content: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures how long it takes for the main content of a web page to appear. This is important because it helps users know when the page is ready. A good LCP time is 2.5 seconds or less. It tracks the largest visible element, like images, videos, or text blocks. A faster LCP improves user experience and helps websites perform better.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Preload the largest image to improve LCP:

<link rel="preload" as="image" href="hero-image.jpg">

userStories.title: As a user visiting the site, I want the main content to load quickly so that I can start engaging with the page without waiting.

As a commuter on the BART train riding through Lafayette, I want your website to display the main content efficiently so my temporarily constrained bandwidth which radically exacerbates loading times doesn't make me give up before it loads.

attributes.description: The server sends the first byte of content within 0.8 seconds.
attributes.why: Measures how quickly a server responds, impacting page load speed and user experience by reducing wait times.
attributes.risk: Slow server delays everything on your page.
attributes.error: Server response took over 0.8 seconds.

userStories.title: As a user requesting a web page, I want the server to respond quickly so that the page starts loading without noticeable delay.

about.title: About
about.content: Time to First Byte (TTFB) measures the time it takes from when a browser requests a resource to when the first byte of data begins to load. It's an important metric for website performance, affecting how quickly a page appears. A good TTFB is under 0.8 seconds, and anything over 1.8 seconds needs improvement. TTFB is influenced by server responsiveness and connection setup time. Lowering it can improve overall user experience and speed.

code.title: Code
code.content:
nginx — enable gzip compression and caching to reduce TTFB:

gzip on;
gzip_types text/plain text/css application/json application/javascript;

location ~* \.(css|js|png|jpg|svg|woff2)$ {
  expires 1y;
  add_header Cache-Control "public, immutable";
}

videos.title: Time to First Byte (TTFB)
videos.description: Using ScanGov to audit performance, how the scangov.com site itself performs on the performance metric time to first byte.

attributes.description: The site has a name for social media and browser display.
attributes.why: Sets the website's title for social media sharing, ensuring consistent branding and better previews when content is shared.
attributes.risk: Site name missing in social media shares.
attributes.error: Open Graph site name is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph site name tag helps define the name of the website when content is shared on social media platforms. This tag is important for consistency in branding across social media and ensures that the website is properly identified when users share content. Without it, the default title might be used, which can lead to incorrect or incomplete branding.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:site_name" content="Agency Name">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want rich and visually appealing previews when a website is shared on social media so that I can easily understand the content and decide whether to click on the link.

attributes.description: The page declares its content type for social media cards.
attributes.why: Specifies content type for social media, improving how links are displayed and enhancing engagement when shared online.
attributes.risk: Social media shows wrong content type.
attributes.error: Open Graph type is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph type tag specifies the type of content being shared (e.g., website, article, video, etc.) and helps social media platforms understand how to render the content. It allows for better control over how the shared content is presented, ensuring that the correct type of preview is displayed, which enhances user engagement. Without this tag, the content may not display optimally on social platforms.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:type" content="website">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the content shared from the website to display the correct type of information (e.g., article, video, website) so that I can easily understand the context and purpose of the link before clicking on it.

attributes.description: The page has a title for social media previews.
attributes.why: Sets a clear title for shared content, ensuring accurate and engaging previews on social media platforms.
attributes.risk: Shared links have no title on social media.
attributes.error: Open Graph title is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph title tag defines the title of the page when it is shared on social media platforms. It ensures that the title appears clearly and accurately on platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn. Without a well-defined Open Graph title, social media platforms may default to the page's title or pick a less relevant part of the page content, leading to poor user engagement and a less polished appearance on social media.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:title" content="Page Title — Agency Name">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the title of the shared content to be clear and descriptive so that I can easily understand what the link is about before clicking on it.

attributes.description: The page has a description for social media previews.
attributes.why: Provides a brief summary of content, helping users understand what the link is about when shared on social media.
attributes.risk: Shared links have no description on social media.
attributes.error: Open Graph description is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph description tag specifies the description that will be shown when the page is shared on social media platforms. It plays a crucial role in providing a concise and engaging summary of the page's content. Without a well-defined Open Graph description, social platforms may default to content from the page itself, which could lead to inaccurate or unappealing descriptions, potentially reducing click-through rates and user engagement on social media.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:description" content="A brief description of this page.">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the description of shared content to be informative and concise so that I can quickly understand what the content is about before deciding to click the link.

attributes.description: The page has a canonical URL for social media sharing.
attributes.why: Defines the URL of the shared content, ensuring correct linking and improving how the page is displayed on social media.
attributes.risk: Shared links may point to wrong page.
attributes.error: Canonical link missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph URL tag provides the canonical URL for the content, which is essential for social media platforms to know which page to link to when shared. If the URL is missing or incorrect, social media platforms may not correctly associate the content with the intended page, potentially causing issues like broken links or the sharing of the wrong page, which could affect user engagement and SEO performance.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:url" content="https://example.gov/page/">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the shared content to link to the correct webpage, so that I can be sure I am clicking the right link and accessing the intended content.

attributes.description: The page has a preview image for social media cards.
attributes.why: Sets an image for social media previews, improving engagement by displaying relevant visuals when content is shared.
attributes.risk: Shared links have no image on social media.
attributes.error: Open Graph image is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph image tag is used to specify a preview image that appears when a page is shared on social media. Without this tag, social media platforms may not display any image, or they might display an irrelevant image, which could negatively affect user engagement and click-through rates. Including a proper Open Graph image ensures that the page looks attractive and engaging when shared on platforms like Facebook, X, and LinkedIn.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:image" content="https://example.gov/assets/share-image.jpg">

userStories.title: As a social media user, I want shared links to have a clear and relevant image so that I can easily understand the content of the link before clicking on it.

attributes.description: The social media preview image has alt text for screen readers.
attributes.why: Provides a description for the image, improving accessibility and helping search engines understand the image content.
attributes.risk: Screen readers cannot describe shared images.
attributes.error: Open Graph image alt is missing.

about.title: About
about.content: The Open Graph image alt tag provides alternative text for the image specified in the Open Graph protocol. This helps improve accessibility for users with visual impairments, as screen readers can describe the image content. Additionally, providing alt text ensures that the content of the image is still conveyed in cases where the image cannot be loaded or viewed, contributing to a better user experience and broader reach.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Add inside <head>:

<meta property="og:image:alt" content="Description of the share image">

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the image shared on social media to have an alt text so that I can understand the content of the image even if I can't see it, especially if I rely on screen readers.

security.description: Secure your site and protect users with proper security practices.
security.standardsDescription: Standards for protecting your website and its users from online threats.
security.guidanceDescription: Policy and guidance that supports website security and data protection.
security.why: Strong defenses prevent hackers from stealing personal information and other confidential data.
security.risk: Hackers can steal private information from your site.

attributes.description: The site has a policy that limits what external content can load on its pages.
attributes.why: Helps stop hackers by blocking harmful code from running on your website.
attributes.risk: Hackers can inject bad code into your site.
attributes.error: Content Security Policy is missing or too permissive.

userStories.title: As a website owner, I want to define a Content Security Policy so that I can prevent malicious scripts from executing and protect users from XSS attacks.
As a site visitor, I want the website to load safely and securely without being exposed to malicious content or scripts so that my browsing experience is safe and my data is protected.

about.title: About
about.content:
Content Security Policy (CSP) is a security standard that helps protect websites from attacks like cross-site scripting (XSS) by allowing website owners to declare trusted sources of content.

CSP is delivered using HTTP headers or HTML meta tags, and uses directives like default-src, script-src, and img-src to control resource loading. CSP can enforce policies, report violations, and is not a replacement for careful input validation, but a defense-in-depth measure. Strict CSP is more secure using nonces or hashes.

Note: It's an extra layer of protection, not the primary defense.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example header:

Content-Security-Policy:
  default-src 'self';
  script-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
  img-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  font-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  object-src 'none';
  frame-ancestors 'none';
  upgrade-insecure-requests;

Example HTML code:

<!-- Content Security Policy example using meta tag -->
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="
  default-src 'self';
  script-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
  img-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  font-src 'self' https://example.gov;
  object-src 'none';
  frame-ancestors 'none';
  upgrade-insecure-requests;
">

links.title: Links
links.content:
• Content Security Policy (18F)
  • Reining in the Web with Content Security Policy (Mozilla)
  • Content Security Policy (Mozilla)
  • Content Security Policy Level 3 (W3C)
  • Content Security Policy (Wikipedia)
  • Content Security Policy Cheat Sheet (OWASP)

example.title: Example
example.content:
A minimal but effective CSP for a government site that uses only its own assets:

Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: https:; font-src 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'

Progressively tighten by switching from Content-Security-Policy to report-only mode first:

Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only: default-src 'self'; report-uri https://example.gov/csp-report

attributes.description: The site forces an encrypted HTTPS connection for all users.
attributes.why: Forces secure connections, protecting user data by making websites always load with encryption.
attributes.risk: Hackers can downgrade your connection and steal data.
attributes.error: Site doesn't force secure connection; easier for hackers to intercept.

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the website to automatically redirect to HTTPS and prevent any connection via HTTP so that my data is always encrypted and secure during transmission.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content:
All government websites must have HSTS.

M-15-13:

Strict Transport Security: Websites and services available over HTTPS must enable HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)12 to instruct compliant browsers to assume HTTPS going forward. This reduces the number of insecure redirects, and protects users against attacks that attempt to downgrade connections to plain HTTP. Once HSTS is in place, domains can be submitted to a “preload list”13 used by all major browsers to ensure the HSTS policy is in effect at all times.

CIO.gov:

The policy should be deployed at https://domain.gov, not https://www.domain.gov.

about.title: About
about.content:
HTTP Strict Transport Security is a security feature that:

  • Forces web browsers to use HTTPS instead of HTTP.
  • Protects against downgrade attacks and cookie hijacking.
  • Specifies a period during which the browser should enforce HTTPS for the site.

Key points:

  • Activated by the server through a response header (Strict-Transport-Security).
  • Helps improve website security by ensuring encrypted connections.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example HTTP response header:

Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload

links.title: Links
links.content:
• HTTP Strict Transport Security (CIO.gov)
  • HTTP Strict Transport Security (Mozilla)
  • Security-related HTTP headers (Cloud.gov)
  • HTTP Strict Transport Security Cheat Sheet (OWASP)
  • HTTP Strict Transport Security (Wikipedia)

attributes.description: The site has a security.txt file so researchers know how to report issues.
attributes.why: Provides contact info for reporting security issues, helping site owners fix problems quickly and keep users safe.
attributes.risk: Security researchers cannot report problems to you.
attributes.error: No available security.txt file.

about.title: About
about.content:
security.txt is a text file located on the site root-level or `/.well-known/` directory that helps improve vulnerability disclosure by giving security researchers clear contact and reporting information.

security.txt is an accepted standard (RFC 9116) by the Internet Engineering Task Force.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Place at /.well-known/security.txt:

Contact: mailto:security@agency.gov
Expires: 2026-12-31T23:59:59.000Z
Preferred-Languages: en
Canonical: https://agency.gov/.well-known/security.txt

example.title: Example
example.content:
A complete security.txt file with all recommended fields:

# U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs — security.txt
# https://www.va.gov/.well-known/security.txt

Contact: mailto:security@va.gov
Expires: 2026-12-31T23:59:59.000Z
Encryption: https://www.va.gov/pgp-key.txt
Acknowledgments: https://www.va.gov/security/hall-of-fame/
Preferred-Languages: en
Canonical: https://www.va.gov/.well-known/security.txt
Policy: https://www.va.gov/security/vulnerability-disclosure-policy/
Hiring: https://www.va.gov/careers/

userStories.title: As a security researcher, I want to find a security.txt file on the website so that I can report vulnerabilities to the right contact securely and efficiently.

attributes.description: The site tells browsers not to guess the type of files it serves.
attributes.why: Stops browsers from guessing file types, helping prevent security risks by enforcing correct content handling.
attributes.risk: Browsers might run dangerous files by mistake.
attributes.error: Missing or wrong X-Content-Type-Options setting.

about.title: About
about.content:
`X-Content-Type-Options` is a security header that:

  • Prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing.
  • Ensures content is rendered as declared (e.g., no misinterpretation of file types).
  • Stops browsers from guessing content types, enhancing security.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Example HTTP response header:

X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff

attributes.description: The page loads without JavaScript or network errors.
attributes.why: Signals problems in website code, helping developers fix issues that could affect how the site works or displays.
attributes.risk: Site may have hidden bugs that break features.
attributes.error: Page load problem — check browser console for errors and details.

userStories.title: As a security researcher, I want to find a security.txt file on the website so that I can report vulnerabilities to the right contact securely and efficiently.

about.title: About
about.content: Errors logged to the console help developers find and fix problems in their code. When something goes wrong, like a bug or incorrect function, the console displays a message with details about the issue. This helps developers understand what went wrong, where it happened, and what needs to be fixed. By using the console, developers can troubleshoot and improve their websites or applications, making them run more smoothly for users.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Failing pattern:

// Fail: accessing a property on undefined throws a console error
const name = user.profile.name;

Passing pattern:

// Pass: guard against undefined before accessing nested properties
const name = user?.profile?.name ?? "";

links.title: Links
links.content: • Browser errors were logged to the console (Google Lighthouse)

attributes.description: The site prevents other websites from embedding its pages to trick users into clicking.
attributes.why: Prevents malicious websites from tricking users into clicking hidden elements, enhancing site security and protecting user actions.
attributes.risk: Hackers can trick users into clicking wrong things.
attributes.error: Page is not protected from possible clickjacking by containing frames.

userStories.title: As a user, I want to safely click buttons and links so I'm not tricked into doing something I didn't mean to.

about.title: About
about.content:
Clickjacking is a malicious attack where users are tricked into clicking on links or user interface elements on a site that appears to be a trusted and familiar site. This is typically accomplished by embedding part or all of the trusted site into the malicious site using an <iframe>.

The X-Frame-Options (XFO) header and the frame-ancestors directive in the Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header can mitigate clickjacking attacks by controlling how a site can be embedded within an $lt;iframe>.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Option 1 — X-Frame-Options header:

X-Frame-Options: DENY

Option 2 — Content Security Policy (preferred):

Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none'

attributes.description: The page lets users paste content into form fields.
attributes.why: Negatively impacts user experience and weakens security by blocking password managers.
attributes.risk: Users cannot use password managers safely.
attributes.error: Page doesn't allow copy-paste into inputs.

about.title: About
about.content:
Some websites claim that allowing users to paste passwords reduces security. However, password pasting actually improves security because it enables the use of password managers.

Password managers typically generate strong passwords for users, store them securely, and then automatically paste them into password fields whenever users need to log in. This approach is generally more secure than forcing users to type in passwords that are short enough to remember.

In the general case, users should not be prevented from pasting into <input> elements.

code.title: Code
code.content:
Correct input — allows paste:

<input type="text" autocomplete="current-password">

Incorrect — blocks paste:

<input type="text" onpaste="return false;">

links.title: Links
links.content: • Prevents users from pasting into input fields

attributes.description: The site uses HTTPS to encrypt data between the server and users.
attributes.why: Secures data by encrypting communication, protecting user privacy and preventing unauthorized access to sensitive information during online interactions.
attributes.risk: Hackers can see and steal personal information.
attributes.error: Site is not served over HTTPS.

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want the website to use HTTPS so that my data is encrypted and protected from interception or tampering while browsing or submitting information.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content:
All government websites must have HTTPS.

OMB Memorandum M-15-1338 requires agencies to encrypt HTTP traffic that travels over the public internet to or from a Federal system, using HTTPS and HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)

about.title: About
about.content:
Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure (HTTPS) is the strongest privacy and integrity protection currently available for public web connections. HTTPS ensures users that their privacy is protected when visiting a government website.

HTTPS is indicated by a lock icon and/or https:// in the browser bar.

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example government websites with HTTPS:

  • https://digital.gov
  • https://whitehouse.gov

links.title: Links
links.content:
• The HTTPS-Only Standard (CIO.gov)
  • M-15-13: Policy to Require Secure Connections across Federal Websites and Web Services

code.title: Code
code.content:
nginx redirect HTTP to HTTPS:

server {
  listen 80;
  server_name example.gov www.example.gov;
  return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

attributes.description: The site uses a government-issued domain (.gov, .edu, or .mil).
attributes.why: Defines trusted website categories, helping users identify official sites and improving credibility and security for specific organizations.
attributes.risk: People cannot tell if your site is real government.
attributes.error: Domain is not an official sTLD.

userStories.title: As a site visitor, I want to access government websites with a `.gov` domain so that I can be confident the site is official, secure, and trustworthy, providing accurate and authoritative government information.

guidance.title: Guidance
guidance.content: All government websites must have a .gov / .edu / .mil sponsored top-level domain.

about.title: About
about.content:
Generic top-level domains (gTLD) (.com, .org, etc.) are non-country extensions that indicate the purpose or source of the website.

sTLDs (.gov / .edu / .mil) are a subgroup of gTLDs managed by designated organizations and restricted to specific registrant types.

A .gov / .edu / .mil extension verifies that the website is managed by a United States government organization (federal, state, local). Government non-.gov gTLDs can potentially confuse users and create opportunities for non-government entities to spoof official government services. Adopting the .gov / .edu / .mil extension ensures users are visiting an official government website.

example.title: Examples
example.content:
Example of government websites with a .gov sTLD:

  • digital.gov
  • whitehouse.gov

links.title: Links
links.content:
• .gov (Wikipedia)
  • Get a .gov (Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency)
  • Sponsored top-level domain (Wikipedia)
